title: Central Bank Bond Purchases and the Price of Safety
gist: SF Fed researchers find that ECB bond purchases (2015–2021, totaling ~30% of euro-area GDP) materially lowered the safety premiums on highly-rated government bonds—by as much as 30 basis points in total—as central bank reserves replaced risky bonds, expanding the supply of safe assets and partially offsetting the very yield declines the purchases aimed to achieve.

themes.theme: ECB QE Reduced Safety Premiums, Partially Offsetting Its Own Yield Impact

insights.claim: When a central bank buys risky high-yield bonds using safe central bank reserves, it increases the overall supply of safe assets in the economy—which lowers the 'safety premium' investors pay for high-quality government bonds, putting upward pressure on safe bond yields and partially offsetting the intended yield reduction.
insights.speaker: Jens H. E. Christensen
insights.so_what: Central bank QE programs that buy risky assets using reserves are less effective at lowering safe bond yields than models assuming no safety premium effects suggest—policymakers must account for this partial offset when calibrating asset purchase programs.
insights.quote: Central banks generally use bond purchases to reduce yields. However, paying for bonds using their own reserves, which are the safest and most liquid assets, has the potential to raise the amount of safe assets in the economy.
insights.locator: Central banks generally use bond purchases

insights.claim: ECB bond purchases 2015–2021 totaled about 30% of euro-area nominal GDP; each 1 percentage point of ECB purchases (as share of GDP) is associated with approximately 1 basis point decline in safety premiums across Germany, Denmark, Sweden, and Switzerland—implying cumulative impact of up to 30 basis points total.
insights.speaker: Jens H. E. Christensen
insights.so_what: 30 basis points of safety premium compression is economically significant (roughly half the average 66 bp safety premium in the sample); this quantifies a material, cross-border spillover of ECB QE that affected even non-euro countries with independent currencies (Sweden, Switzerland).
insights.quote: an increase in the ECB bond purchases equal to 1 percentage point of nominal GDP in the euro area is associated with an average decline in the safety premium across the four countries of about 1 basis point
insights.locator: The results shown in the first data point

insights.claim: Germany has the highest average safety premium among the four studied AAA countries at 124 basis points, followed by Switzerland (70 bps), Sweden (54 bps), and Denmark (16 bps)—reflecting each country's safe haven status and integration with the euro area.
insights.speaker: Jens H. E. Christensen
insights.so_what: German Bunds carry the largest safety premium in Europe (124 bps)—meaning Bund yields are structurally depressed by 124 bps relative to non-safe-haven equivalents; as ECB balance sheet shrinks (QT), safety premiums should expand, pushing Bund yields higher absent other factors.
insights.quote: Germany has the highest safety premium among the four countries with an average of 124 basis points (hundredths of a percentage point), followed by Switzerland at 70 basis points and Sweden at 54.
insights.locator: Figure 1 shows the estimated safety premiums

insights.claim: The findings imply an important international spillover: ECB bond purchases lowered safety premiums not just in Germany but also in non-euro Sweden, Switzerland, and Denmark—suggesting global safe asset supply effects that cross currency and regulatory borders.
insights.speaker: Jens H. E. Christensen
insights.so_what: QE by major central banks affects safe asset pricing globally, not just domestically—a key consideration for investors in non-euro safe government bonds when the ECB or Fed expands its balance sheet; the cross-border safety premium channel is now empirically confirmed.
insights.quote: These findings point to an important international spillover of central bank bond purchases: The impact on the relative scarcity of safe assets partially offsets the decline in yields for other safe government bond markets.
insights.locator: These findings point to an important international spillover

source_meta.author: Jens H. E. Christensen, Nikola Mirkov, Xin Zhang
